pattern: Breadth-first search over an augmented state of position plus the bitmask of collected keys, since the same cell may be revisited with a different key set and the goal is the first time every key bit is set.
hints: Your state is not just where you stand, but also which keys you already hold, because a locked door becomes passable once you own its key.
Encode the set of collected keys as a bitmask and treat (row, column, keymask) as a single search node.
Run breadth-first search from the start with an empty keymask; expand to neighbors, skipping walls and locks whose key bit is unset, and stop when the keymask equals all-keys-present.

edge_inputs.description: must collect a key before a lock blocks the path
two keys reachable around a wall
a lock with its key behind it is unreachable
a single adjacent key, one move
straight corridor collecting keys in order before locks
wall fully isolates the only key
key requires a detour through an opened door

stress.description: random small grids with one key/lock pair placed so the start, key, and lock are distinct reachable-ish cells
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    rows = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 6)))
    cols = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 6)))
    cells = [[ '.' for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]
    # sprinkle some walls
    for r in range(rows):
        for c in range(cols):
            if rng.random() < 0.2:
                cells[r][c] = '#'
    coords = [(r, c) for r in range(rows) for c in range(cols)]
    rng.shuffle(coords)
    picks = coords[:3]
    sr, sc = picks[0]
    kr, kc = picks[1]
    lr, lc = picks[2]
    cells[sr][sc] = '@'
    cells[kr][kc] = 'a'
    cells[lr][lc] = 'A'
    return [[''.join(row) for row in cells]]
stress.note: exactly one '@', one key 'a', one lock 'A'; result may be -1 when blocked, which is a valid answer
